title: Workbook — Creation and Providence
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Section 1 — Remembering the Essentials

  1. What does it mean that God created the world «out of nothing» (creatio ex nihilo)? How is this different from the idea that God merely shaped pre-existing matter? What does this truth imply about God's power and freedom?
  2. How do the three Persons of the Trinity — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — each participate in the work of creation? Why is it important that creation is a Trinitarian act and not the work of the Father alone?
  3. What did God declare about his creation in Genesis 1:31, and why is this statement so important? What does it mean to say that the material world — including the human body — is fundamentally good?
  4. What is divine providence? How does it relate to human freedom, and why does God's governance of the universe not cancel out our genuine free choices?
  5. According to the Catechism, why does God permit evil? If God is both all-good and all-powerful, what answer does the Cross give us to this question?
  6. Who are the angels, and what role do they play in God's plan of creation and salvation? Why are they not to be worshipped?

Section 2 — Deepening Your Understanding

2.1 Creatio Ex Nihilo and the Ancient World

In the ancient Near East, many cultures told creation stories in which gods battled sea monsters and shaped the world from the bodies of defeated enemies (for example, the Babylonian Enuma Elish). How does the biblical account of creation differ from these stories? What does the difference tell us about who God is and what creation means? Write a paragraph comparing the two visions.

 

 

 

 

 

 

2.2 The Six Days and the Sabbath Rest

Read Genesis 1:1 — 2:3 carefully. Notice the pattern: God speaks, separation happens, new things fill the space. On the seventh day, God rests. The Catechism says that the seventh day «completes the first creation» (CCC 345). If God doesn't get tired, why does he rest? What is the Sabbath trying to teach us about the purpose of creation and the meaning of human work? How does the rhythm of work and rest shape a healthy spiritual life?

 

 

 

 

 

2.3 Angels: Messengers and Guardians

Look up three passages in Scripture where angels appear: the angel stopping Abraham (Gen 22:11-12), the archangel Gabriel announcing the Incarnation (Lk 1:26-38), and the angel strengthening Jesus in Gethsemane (Lk 22:43). For each passage, answer: what does the angel do? What does this action reveal about how God works through his messengers? Now reflect: do you have a relationship with your guardian angel? What would change if you consciously asked for their help each morning?

 

 

 

 

 

 

2.4 Providence and Human Freedom: The Two-Level Dance

The Catechism teaches that God is the First Cause who works through secondary causes — including our free choices (CCC 306-308). Draw a diagram showing how God's universal causality and human freedom coexist without competing. Label what comes from God (existence, the power to act, the laws of nature) and what comes from creatures (particular choices, human actions, natural causes). Now explain in a paragraph: why does God's sovereignty not reduce us to puppets?

 

 

 

 

 

2.5 The Problem of Evil: The Cross as Answer

Saint Augustine taught that evil is not a thing but a privation of good — something missing where good ought to be, like blindness in an eye or injustice in a human action. Read CCC 309-314. In your own words, explain: (a) why God is not the author of moral evil, (b) why he permits it anyway, and (c) how the Cross demonstrates that evil does not have the final word. Is there a particular suffering in your own life (or the life of someone you know) that you need to entrust to God's providence? Write a brief prayer about it.

 

 

 

 

 

 

2.6 Science and Faith: The Two Books of God

Georges Lemaître was both a Catholic priest and the physicist who first proposed the Big Bang theory. Read about his life and his insistence that science and faith answer different questions. Then answer: (a) What question does science answer about the universe? (b) What question does faith answer? (c) Why can a Catholic accept the Big Bang and biological evolution as scientific theories while still affirming that God is the Creator of everything? Use the Catechism's teaching (CCC 283-289) to support your answer.

 

 

 

 

 

Section 3 — For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no «correct» answers to these personal questions.

1. Think of something beautiful you have seen today — a tree, a cloud, someone's face. How does it change the way you see it knowing that God created it out of love and sustains it at this very moment?

Don't think of something distant. Look at what is close to you. Creation is not only the stars; it is also what is right in front of you.

 

 

 

2. If creation is the work of the entire Trinity — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — how does that change the way you relate to the material world?

Do you see the world as something «neutral» or as something that bears the mark of Trinitarian love?

 

 

 

3. Do you find it hard to believe that the material world — and your own body — is «very good» (Gen 1:31)? Why or why not?

Sometimes we think that the spiritual is good and the material is bad. Where does that idea come from, and what does the Church actually teach?

 

 

 

4. How do you reconcile God's providence with the suffering you see around you and the suffering you have experienced yourself? What does the Cross say to you about this?

Don't look for a perfect answer. Write what you feel, what is difficult, what you hope for. Faith does not explain evil; it trusts in the midst of it.

 

 

 

5. Look around you right now. Can you see something that reminds you that God loves you and sustains your existence at this moment?

Name something concrete. Faith is lived in the details. The God who sustains galaxies also sustains the room you are sitting in.

 

 

 

Section 4 — Practical Activities

A. Creation Walk

Go for a 15-minute walk. With each step, name aloud something God created: the sky, the plants, the air, the birds, the people. Pause at least three times to look closely at something small — the veins on a leaf, the texture of bark, the movement of an insect. When you return, write a brief prayer of thanksgiving for the specific things you noticed.

 

 

 

 

 

B. Reading the Poetry of Creation

Read Psalm 104 aloud slowly, underlining every verb that describes God's action in creation: «stretch out,» «cover,» «make,» «give,» «renew.» Then do the same with Jeremiah 10:12-13. What kind of God emerges from these poems — a distant architect or a present, active, tender sustainer? Write a paragraph describing how these poems make you feel about the Creator.

 

 

 

 

 

C. Letter to the Creator

Write a letter to God starting with «Thank you for creating me...» Include three things: what you admire most about creation, something about creation that is hard for you to understand, and a specific concern or person you want to entrust to his providence. This is not an essay; it is a conversation. Write as you would speak to someone who loves you.

 

 

 

 

 

 

D. The Canticle of the Creatures

Read Saint Francis of Assisi's Canticle of the Creatures (easily found online). Notice how Francis addresses the elements — Brother Sun, Sister Moon, Sister Water — as members of a single family. Now write your own short canticle. Address at least five elements of creation as brother or sister and give thanks to God through each one. Try to include both grand things (the sun, the stars) and small, ordinary things (the soil, the water, the bread on your table).

 

 

 

 

 

 

E. Science and Faith Journal

Find a recent scientific discovery or news story about the natural world — a new species, a medical breakthrough, a finding about the cosmos. Read about it. Then write a one-page journal entry answering: (a) What does this discovery tell me about how the universe works? (b) What does it tell me about the Creator who designed it? (c) Does this discovery make my faith stronger or more difficult? Why?

 

 

 

 

 

Section 5 — Memory Verse

«You love all things that exist, and you hate none of the things you have made, for you would not have made anything if you had hated it. How would anything have endured if you had not willed it? Or how would anything not called forth by you have been preserved? You spare all things, for they are yours, O Lord, you who love the living.»

— Wisdom 11:24-26

Memory tip: Write this passage by hand three times this week. Speak it aloud once each morning. Underline «love,» «spare,» «preserved,» and «yours.»

Section 6 — Prayer for This Week

Lord, you who created the world out of nothing
and sustain it with your love at every moment,
open my eyes to see your imprint in everything around me.
Help me to trust in your providence,
even when I do not understand the path,
even when suffering obscures your face.
May I know that I do not exist by accident or by chance,
but because you loved me before I drew my first breath,
and because your love sustains my life in every instant.
Teach me to be a good steward of your creation,
to walk lightly on the earth,
to see in every creature a brother or sister,
and to rest in the peace of knowing
that nothing escapes your fatherly care.
Amen.

Print Workbook
